title: Wireless Technologies Compared: WiFi · Bluetooth LE · Classic Bluetooth

zones.label: WiFi
zones.summary: Internet-connected wireless via a router — browser control from any device
facts: Standard: IEEE 802.11 b/g/n — 2.4 GHz only (no 5 GHz on CYW43439)
Hardware on Pico W: Infineon CYW43439 built-in chip (same chip as BLE)
Connection: joins an existing access point (router); gets IP address via DHCP
Range: 15–30 m typical indoors (walls and interference reduce range significantly)
Speed: 10–100 Mbps (more than enough for robot control pages)
Power draw: up to ~270 mA peak TX; ~50–80 mA when connected and idle
MicroPython: import network; WLAN object; connect(), isconnected(), ifconfig()
Robot use: serve an HTML control page on port 80; steer robot from any browser
Requires: access point (router) in range; WiFi credentials in secrets.py file
Best for: web-based teleoperation, IoT data logging, multi-device control

zones.label: Bluetooth LE
zones.summary: Direct device-to-device link — ultra-low power, robot-to-robot without a router
facts: Standard: Bluetooth 5.2 Low Energy (BLE), 2.4 GHz ISM band
Hardware on Pico W: same CYW43439 chip as WiFi — both can run simultaneously via antenna time-sharing
Connection: one device advertises; the other scans and connects — no router needed
Range: 10–30 m outdoors; 5–20 m typical indoors
Speed: 1–3 Mbps (plenty for sensor data and movement commands)
Power draw: very low — designed for coin-cell devices; 1–15 mA active
Round-trip latency: ~1–10 ms (faster than WiFi for direct commands)
MicroPython: import bluetooth; BLE object; advertise(), scan(), connect()
GATT: services and characteristics structure data; central/peripheral roles
Robot use: one Pico W leads (central), another follows (peripheral) — swarm robotics
Best for: robot-to-robot communication, wearables, direct phone-to-robot control

zones.label: Classic Bluetooth
zones.summary: Older wireless standard — CYW43439 has hardware support but official MicroPython exposes BLE only
facts: Standard: Bluetooth BR/EDR (Basic Rate/Enhanced Data Rate), 2.4 GHz ISM band
Hardware: CYW43439 chip has BR/EDR hardware — but Raspberry Pi's official MicroPython firmware exposes BLE only (Classic BT requires C SDK)
Connection: device pairing with PIN; master/slave architecture
Range: 10–100 m (Class 1 up to 100 m; Class 2 typical ~10 m)
Speed: 1 Mbps (BR) to 3 Mbps (EDR); up to 24 Mbps with 802.11 assist
Power draw: medium — higher than BLE; not optimized for battery-powered devices
Round-trip latency: ~30–100 ms (higher than BLE due to polling interval)
Profiles: SPP (serial port), A2DP (audio), HID (keyboard/mouse), RFCOMM
Common uses: wireless speakers, headsets, keyboard/mouse, older Arduino HC-05 robots
Robot use in this course: not used — use BLE (Chapter 12) for direct wireless links
Best for: audio streaming, legacy serial-port replacement if you use the C SDK

quiz.question: Which wireless technology on the Pico W lets you control your robot from any web browser on the same network?
quiz.explanation: WiFi connects the Pico W to a router so it can serve an HTML page on port 80. Any browser on the same network — phone, tablet, laptop — can load the page and send motor commands via HTTP GET or POST.

quiz.question: Which technology uses GATT services and central/peripheral roles to link two robots directly without a router?
quiz.explanation: Bluetooth LE uses the GATT protocol with one device as the central (leader) and the other as the peripheral (follower). They connect directly — no router or access point is needed, and round-trip latency is just 1–10 ms.

quiz.question: Which technology is available in official Raspberry Pi MicroPython firmware only via the C SDK, not through the high-level bluetooth module?
quiz.explanation: The CYW43439 chip has Classic Bluetooth (BR/EDR) hardware, but Raspberry Pi's official MicroPython firmware only exposes BLE. To use BR/EDR you need the lower-level C SDK. In this course we always use BLE, not Classic Bluetooth.

quiz.question: Which wireless technology uses the least power and is designed for coin-cell battery devices with a latency of just 1–10 ms?
quiz.explanation: Bluetooth Low Energy was designed specifically for very low power — it can run on a small coin cell for months by sleeping between transmissions. BLE also has lower latency (1–10 ms) than WiFi for direct robot commands.

quiz.question: Which technology requires a network password stored in a secrets.py file and an access point (router) in range?
quiz.explanation: WiFi needs an access point (router) to join and uses a network password. In this course we store the SSID and password in a secrets.py file that is listed in .gitignore so credentials are never committed to version control.
